title: Grimston Hill Wellington MS471 Memorial
cause_of_loss: Starboard engine failure; stall on attempted forced landing
narrative: Returning from a cross-country training exercise, the Wellington's starboard engine failed. Pilot P/O Adams, unable to maintain height on one engine, attempted a forced landing but stalled while trying to clear telegraph poles, crashing into a cornfield. Police Sergeant Ernest John Thomas Flinders, who happened to be nearby on leave, sprinted across fields and pulled crew members from the burning wreckage despite exploding ammunition. He was awarded the British Empire Medal. F/O Frank Graham-Bell was a distinguished painter and art critic for the New Statesman. Canadian P/O Herbert Ibbott has a lake in northern Manitoba named in his honour.

crew.name: Walter Waldron Adams
crew.fate_detail: Killed when the Wellington stalled while trying to clear telegraph poles during a forced landing attempt. Adams had only 18 hours of daytime Wellington flying experience and no recorded night experience.
crew.bio: A 24-year-old trainee pilot with limited Wellington experience. He was unable to maintain height on one engine after the starboard engine failed.

crew.name: Frank Graham-Bell
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash. Graham-Bell was a distinguished painter and art critic for the New Statesman magazine, a notable cultural loss.
crew.bio: Frank Graham-Bell (1910-1943) was a distinguished painter and art critic who wrote for the New Statesman. At 32, he was the eldest crew member. His death represented a significant loss to the British art world.

crew.name: Herbert Ibbott
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash. Ibbott was a Canadian whose memory lives on -- a lake in northern Manitoba is named in his honour.
crew.bio: A 21-year-old Canadian serving with the RCAF. Ibbott Lake in northern Manitoba is named in his memory.

crew.name: Roland Walter Ballauff
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash at Grimston Hill.

crew.name: Peter Baldwin
crew.fate_detail: Killed in the crash. At 19, Baldwin was the youngest crew member.

maintainer.notes: Memorial at former RAF Ossington site, in Wellow parish area.

external_narratives.summary: Page for P/O Walter Waldron Adams and crew of Wellington MS471 KA:G, 82 OTU. On 9 August 1943, the aircraft's starboard engine failed during a cross-country training exercise. The pilot attempted a forced landing but stalled trying to clear telegraph poles, crashing into a cornfield at Grimston Hill.
external_narratives.key_details: Police Sergeant Ernest Flinders was on leave nearby and pulled crew from the burning wreckage despite exploding ammunition, earning the British Empire Medal. F/O Frank Graham-Bell was a distinguished painter and art critic for the New Statesman. Canadian P/O Herbert Ibbott has a lake in northern Manitoba named in his honour.

external_narratives.summary: Newspaper article marking 80 years since the Wellington MS471 crash at Grimston Hill. Details the heroism of PS Flinders and the stories of the crew, including the notable backgrounds of Graham-Bell (artist/critic) and Ibbott (Canadian whose name lives on in Manitoba geography).
external_narratives.key_details: 80th anniversary commemoration. Pilot Adams had only 18 hours of daytime Wellington flying experience and no night experience recorded. Memorial at former RAF Ossington site dedicated in 2022.

local_contacts.role: Local authority responsible for memorial upkeep
